fr:mainmatter.html:p: The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:title.text: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:title.text: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: coherent remainder as a path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherent remainder as a path object

html:li.html:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:

fr:title.text: path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:title.text: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
